speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This interview with Glenn Gilder was recorded on June 17, 1975.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Glenn Gilder was recorded on June 17, 1975 with Sam Schrager. It began at his home near Troy and was continued at Sam Schrager's home.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, by all I remember is that we hauled our wheat in there, dumped it, took our empty sacks home and refilled them. Well, most of the warehouse, when you hauled it in, they kept the sacks. Because they had to, you couldn't, it was before the days of elevator and long before. But there they did have bins and they kept it. Now I don't know whether anybody ever run that before Arch Dad did or not. That's before my time, but his dad run that mill there for years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he run on water power?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: All together, they didn't have anything else. I think in the later years that they did convert it to electricity, but I never seen that. I had gone from Palouse before they did it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have all the power they needed to do all the grinding they wanted to do?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They seemed to. They had a big dam there, 14 feet high I guess. Maybe higher.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: That pond used to go back up the river there for a mile or more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have big grinding stones?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, it was a stone mill. Although I never any more than just one time walked up and looked around, but then I didn't understand anything about it. It was all great to me.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But he made good flour. We'd haul our own wheat in there and trade with him.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Take the flour home, a whole year's supply at one shot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much would that be?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: 20 sacks or better. 50 pound sacks. 20, 25. I don't know.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: When you take your wheat in there, you trade them a sack of wheat for a sack of flour. Then you got with that all the brand that came off it, and uh, Freena, all the by -products. You got that back. I didn't want it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: We'd feed part of it to the hogs and part of it to kids and make quite a lot of use out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the charge?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There was no charge. It was straight across trade. One sack of wheat, one sack of flour.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But then, a sack of wheat would make more than a sack of flour. That was all it was to that. He probably, more than likely gave us half.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: The flour that come out of it weighed 150 pounds. And the sack of wheat that went in there weighed about 110, if a good sack, so it's 120.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But we were darn happy to do it because wheat wasn't very high. Neither was flour as far as that goes. It was probably a dollar and a quarter to a dollar and a half.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I don't remember, but it was in that neighborhood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that mill buy a lot of wheat? Or did you just sell them, just trade them?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: No, no, no. We drove in there from north of Kennedy Ford. We generally hauled two teams. I was just a little punk of a kid, but I could drive horses.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: My uncle, or my grandpa, well, most of my uncle, would drive a team ahead of me and then I'd follow them with mine. We had all about 45 sacks to the team. But when we'd get down there, there might be a line of teams lined up there for, oh, clear out across the main road to Potlass. There might be 10 or 15 or 24 horse teams in there. No, they handle a good volume of business. They bought or traded or any way you wanted a deal. They didn't care.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But it made a pretty good deal for the farmers, I guess, because they got the sack back and that was quite an expense. And, uh,

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: you got the market price for the wheat just the same as if they'd kept the sack. The old man was a good businessman and he was a good miller. He knew his stuff. He was a good farmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you think the mill's market would have been for their grain?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, there was a ready market. They would buy it on the open market, Spokane or anyplace else, but I'll just bet you that they put in there and ate that up right around Potlass. I'll just bet that they did.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Because you bring in flour from other areas, it more than likely cost more. It certainly had cost more to produce it and ship it because of the added things that entered in there, but, uh,

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I'll bet you it was almost entirely eaten around Potlass, but I don't know that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Palouse was really a pretty big town to the, important town to the Pot Palouse River area.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, until they put the mill in there, there wasn't anything from Palouse to the head of the river. There was a stage stop and post office and, a few groceries, but very few. Small little stores that weren't, well not near as big as this living room, but they had the post office and store and what have you in there.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There was a stage stop at Kennedy Ford and there was a stage stop next to Princeton, Old Town, Hampton. And then I don't think there was another until you got to Woodfell. And then there was, you had to go over the mountain to Amida and they were all served by the same stage line. And I think he turned around at Amida and came back. I'm not positive for that, but I think he did. And another met him there and went on to St. Mary's in the St. Joe River.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will this be a daily run from Palouse?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I don't believe they made it a daily run. I think about twice a week.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: When the sawmill was being built and come in there, Art Craig was running it, the stage, and there'd be a bunch of men come in to Palouse, you know, and wanted to go up the river for the woods work or railroad or whatever. So Art couldn't take them all, but he charged them just as much to take the pack sack and bed and they were happy to get it done. And then they walked and the wagon hauled their stuff. And plus the girls wasn't male. Female passengers, they couldn't walk. Kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You must have had a pretty good business. He did.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He did.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And then after this excitement was all over and the mill would end and the railroad was built, well that ruined it. So he put up a livery barn down in Princeton, that's in the new town, and spent the rest of his life there running that livery stable.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Horses, saddle horses, driving horses, freight horses, anything he wanted, they had it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he start in Princeton or did he start in Potlatch?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, he started in Palouse for the first of it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Whether there was anybody else run a stage lineup on that, I don't really know. Edna could tell you.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: She knew it farther back than I did and she knew it much better because her dad run the post office there at Woodfell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder about how long it would take him to get to Woodfell.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: That was an overnight stop for travelers, but probably not for the... A good team would do it easily, but if it were loaded pretty heavy, well it might be quite a task, but...

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Just an idea that that was an overnight stop right there. The next day they went on to Amida, stayed overnight and came back. It would take four days, three or four days to do that. I can figure I don't, I certainly don't remember. For a while I've heard there was a stage that went up to at least as far as Grizzle Camp, but I thought further up the Palouse River. They did, that's right. They had to, it went from Harvard up the Palouse River to those plaster mines up there, but I don't know who it is. I don't know anything about it, I don't remember a thing about their schedule.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But then there wasn't any other transportation, it was just that horse and there was lots and lots of little feeders, stage lines all over the country. The same as there are highways and railroads now. And the railroads come in and not cut them out of their main line.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Have you ever rode red?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: This shadowy St.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Joe?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've seen it though.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: You could get a lot of help on that book. The first half of it at any rate.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear of the old Hoodoo Trail?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, it went from Johnson's over the hump to the head of the north where they could flue us.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Pack Trail. And by the way, Jake Johnson used to run that pack string in there over that trail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was his place at?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Woodfell. His place was at Woodfell. That was Woodfell. He ran the post office and stayed stopped there. He was enterprising, he was a worker and so was the rest of his family. In fact the matter is, all families were at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did it come from when you met Laura?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It had to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This old Hoodoo Trail, it went up and managed to pass close to a lot of the claims in Hoodoos.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: The claims in the Hoodoos were pretty well bunched on the North Fork.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Whether they had a distributing point up there or not I don't know.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He probably went as far as he had mail or groceries or whatever to go up there and then turn around and come back. You can make that trip up and back in a day.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: You don't know exactly where Woodfell is, do you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh I do, yeah. It's about Harvard there. It went right straight east, right over the hills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Up near Bald Mountain?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yes, I think it probably got that high but maybe not.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I've been over it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: No, I don't think so. Although there was a trail over Bald Mountain. I think it is back to the Blue Stig down lower, right across the head of Strickland Creek.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I've been reading a little bit about Henry Plummer lately.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He really does sound like a big thief.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: A real desperado. He was, no doubt about it. When I was a kid they claimed that those cabins at Laird Park were used by him at different times in his horse business. He had a pretty good horse business.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He drove them both ways.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Alder Gulls in that area in there. To Tico and out on the Spokane area. And then he'd load up another bunch from down here. He'd sell the ones he got in the east and he'd sell them down here and then he'd take another bunch back there and sell them.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They used to claim that that cabin was used by him at different times. We know that he was in and out of Lewiston real frequently so there's little doubt of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it just a straight horse thief operation?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Oh no, he wasn't particular. He'd steal anything. He was going to hunt for a stealing gold.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: No, he wasn't a specialist. He had a varied business.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He wasn't particular. He killed a Chinaman just as quick as he would a white man. I thought he had a couple of dollars or a gold watch on him while he was doomed.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Been a lot written on him. A lot of it good. Jodice Fisher I think had a pretty good book on him. I thought it was good anyway. I don't know what the name of it was. I don't even remember very much about it because it's probably could be 50 years since I read it. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there any other guys in the country who were noted for being...

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, yes, but I don't know if I could put you any names on them. I never knew any of them. As far as I know I didn't know any of them. But it was quite a common thing.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: You see these Indians around here, old Mox and Spokane Gary and all those fellows. They were millionaires and horses.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Colonel George Wright killed out there across this side of the river from Harrison. They killed 800 head up there but that didn't hurt them. They thought they were just putting them out of business. Well, they did put them out of business, all right. They killed 800 head and as far as the horses were concerned, I didn't hurt them. Old Mox had lots of horses. And he pastored them down here at Kenrick, Richville. He had a big outfit there at Spokane Falls.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Or not Spokane Falls, Palouse Falls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is Mox Mox the same Indian who used to be up around Bovill?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yes, but that was only just for berries or honey. It wasn't his territory. He kind of stayed a little farther west because horses was his business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he happen to be so well known?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He was so big in his business. He was honest. There was no horse dealing with him.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And if a man needed a horse and he knew it and he had any respect for the man at all, he'd give him a horse. He didn't think anything of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he took his herds up here in the summertime?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He didn't move them a great deal. He just let them move themselves. He didn't have to. There was always feed for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the horse?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I've heard estimates and read estimates about what he had.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There were a lot of horses. On my dad's homestead down there at La Crosse, there was nothing to see daily. I'd probably see horses that were wild. There wasn't anybody on them. My dad caught possibly ten ahead of them during the time he was there.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He caught one little mare. I don't think she ever did weigh over her.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Oh, possibly 800 pounds.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And bred her to a pretty fair horse. Got a 1 ,000 to 1 ,100 pound colt out of her. And kept right on with that same blood there for a good many years. I don't know how many colts they had with that blood in them. They had others too.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: When he left that homestead down there and came up to Palouse,

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: he sold her and I had a good ball over it. I'd never ridden her or don't think anybody else ever did. They tried it, but it didn't work. But I balled because she was part of the family. She'd been around there five or six years, you know. She'd just been one of the Indian horses. She was wild.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But after the Indians were corralled and put on these reservations, and so badly whipped with that, they just left their horses. They just reverted. They weren't anybody's after that. Because the Indian couldn't claim them. He couldn't take care of them. Half of them weren't even live. So they were just wild horses. And that's where all the wild horses came from. The Indians had the first title to them. One of these Nez Perce Indians, according to the history, went down into Texas and brought two old.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Where they got old. I don't know how old they were, but they brought back two or three horses. That's where the Nez Perce got their horses.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I got that both, well I think that Hatley got it from Doby, but that's where I read it. The best put was from Doby's book.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think we just took the Indians' land away from them? How then? Do you think we just took the Indians' land away from them? Do we do them a real injustice or not?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yes, we certainly did do them an injustice, and we still are. Definitely. But on the other hand,

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: at the time that all the battling was going on, it was needless. The Indians wouldn't have hurt anybody or anything if the white man hadn't showed him how.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: White men, there were always, almost in every Indian uprising, the white men fired the first shots. That was in Nez Perce and just about everyplace else. It was in that massacre there at Whitman. It's the same thing, and it's always been the same thing. The white man was first, and I don't see why anybody could blame an Indian for fighting back.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: What would happen if a Chinaman came in here today and tried to take this country by the same method? Would we just grin and bear it or would we scrap?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And it's the same thing to me.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I wonder, I guess I always will wonder, because I'm too damn stubborn, nobody could prove it to me. But these missionaries come in here to convert the Indian and educate them. They just worship the same God, they just called him by a different name. Well, they called everything else by a different name, that was perfectly natural. That's the way I feel about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seems they divided Nez Perce anyway so badly to have some of them be the heathen and some be the Christian Nez Perce. It seems like it split the tribe right down the middle.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, it did.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Whitman and Spalding came and then a year or two later here come DeSmet and Stevens and they all had a different religion and they all told the Indian a different story.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, gee, who's he going to believe?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I can't see it. I think you've done more harm than good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have much of a reputation back in the early days?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, it was for a while that they did because they had a problem, they had no government. So they organized their own. And they got this hill and a bunch of those fellows in one bunch and then a little later on they made another, they called it Dakota Slim and Blackie, I believe, right after the first one.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And they threw them out of an upper story window of a hotel there in Colfax. And I think that made about a total of about five and that cleaned the thing up. They didn't need them anymore. They just dissolved. Nobody was ever supposed to know who they were or anything about it, but then they did. No question about it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I think it served a purpose there, same as it did with Alder Gulch. They cleaned the place up. And it certainly had to be cleaned up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Alder Gulch?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, that's where they hung the plumber. Yeah.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It really was lawless from what people would say when you were a kid. I mean, it really was my bad. No, when I was a kid, no. They'd gone past that. Well, I mean when they talked about it. That is what it had been. It had. But on the other hand, no. They weren't. There were just these few that thought that they were just a little bit too good to work, so they devised some other means of making a living. But most of them, no.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There was fewer outlaws and fewer crooks in those days, as compared to the honest population, as it is in reverse today. There's more crooks and outlaws and thieves in operation today, and fewer honest people in the high position. It's just the reverse of what it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Things have been getting worse and not better. That's right.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But it's due to the fact that there's more population, there's more money, there's more correspondence, there's more opportunity for it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I read, I think it was Dobie Red said that in the early days, a man could walk out of his cabin, hang his watch on the doorknob, and be gone a year, and come back and it wouldn't have lost a minute, but it would still be there. And nowadays, if you've done that, when you came back, the door wouldn't be there. And likely not the cabin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that was true when you grew up? Could one of your neighbors leave a watch out there?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Oh, I never suppose that happened, but Dobie says as an example.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There was more honesty then, comparably, than there is now. There were fewer people.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Anybody who was crooked or conniving wasn't very popular. He didn't dare.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I know there's two, both of them women, that were just bored to death it says out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were from the east? Would you say most easterners fall in love with this country?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They mostly do.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Mostly. In California, and South, Nevada and Utah and down in there.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: If they ever get a chance, they'll come up here to live and settle if they could make it. So if they could live, most of them only know one way to live, so they gotta stay there until they reach their retirement and then they're too old. If they could, then most of them would prefer this northwest. They must be pretty well ideal. I love the desert and the mountains equally. This prairie palooses every dined inch of it is beautiful. I don't know. If I had been on those covered wagon trains that were going so awful hard and going through so much to get to Oregon, now I drive through Laramie and all that area and get into Fort Hall and down to Boise Valley and up at Vail and up through Baker. I wouldn't have gone through that. I'd have broke my wagon down and stayed there. There's many places that they went through that they couldn't have proved, but I don't care where they went.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Climate -wise, at Boise Valley, as production, Chinaman could have seen it with a cane that it could irrigate from that abundant water there is there. Why they went through it, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you think this country stacks up with places like that?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, it's a mystery to me. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the name of the country?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Salmon, Idaho. That's where they hit the Snake River.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the name of the country?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There were a few dropped off.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: If they went into cattle or sheep or horses, they made tremendous fortunes. If they tried to grow grain, they had one generation to clear it up and get it to production and then it took the next generation to lose it to a bankrupt sale. And that was the case in Yakima and Oneachie valleys. One generation to Homestead, the next generation put it in orchard, and the next generation lost it to foreclosed mortgages.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That didn't happen too bad around here though, did it?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It didn't around right here, but that's just built right into history of all that orchard country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened, do you know?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Oh, the money cost of developing was so big and the slowness of bringing it into actual production, one working against the other, just was too much to drag down through the years.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: A lot of them made it, but they had to work.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They had to have the know to know that they had to get right in and dig. You know, instead of waiting for the orchard to produce, they had to have something producing between them trees, garden stuff or alfalfa hay or eggs, something. And they made it, and they made it good.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Another class that didn't, I don't know what governed it, I have no idea. It seems like around here too, there were a lot of people that left, didn't stay, didn't wind up staying.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know why some didn't, some didn't here either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't have to leave here, they could have managed to make a living, couldn't they?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Most of them had been better off if they had the stick to it enough, lots of perseverance, to hang on to these places a little bit longer, conditions would have got better and they made it. Now there are many of these Bowman family, John Bowman's family, and Hoy dolls over here, they're scattered all over the country that made it and made it darn good. But it'd get tough and they'd think, oh God, I can make four dollars a day, five dollars a day working in the woods or three and a half working in a sawmill and they'd get discouraged and pull out and go and do it. Well if they had of stuck tight to their farm, eventually wound up on the right side of the ledger, I'm sure.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There's one class of people that likes to work for a boss and another class of people that don't want to work for a boss.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Sometimes they don't have the ability to manage it on their own without the boss, but they've still got it in their heart to go that route anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But for a lot of people out here, oh and you were growing up, teens, the twenties, was more money in the cities, wasn't there, than to stay on the farm and work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that's why you would go to Spokane to try to work, because it looked like there'd be money to make there?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: No, I always thought I could make more money out here, but I had to try it, I had to see what it's like, I didn't know. I thought, you know, they had their shows and their dances and lots of excitement, lots of thrills. Well us out here were kind of alone, you know, you weren't, well you weren't working with ten or fifteen people on everyday jobs. Probably we were working where you'd only see two people or three or four, or family, all week and then on Saturday night you might go to a dance or go to town, but it was that difference. I had to try it, that's all. And after I tried it, I wondered, I had all the town life I wanted.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Why?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, about that time there was a dozen of these young guys went to different towns, Spokane, Seattle, Portland, and they're still there. Some of them are wealthy too. Some of them are tremendously wealthy.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But I think I was about the only one that could get my belly full of it in six months.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: The thing that made the biggest impression on me about city life, I had to walk about a mile from where I lived to where I worked, or probably more than a mile. And walk up Main Street and Trent Avenue where I could see all this poverty exemplified in the people on the street. And I think that drove me out more than anything else. I'd sooner be hard up out in the country than in the city. You couldn't be hurt out in the country. If you couldn't get a job, you still could eat. Honestly, you'd get enough jobs to eat and clothe you. Well, town, if they got out of a job, they were out of everything. Next week, because they never could save enough money for next week. And I think that's what built me up on the city. It didn't look good to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of shape were the people in? Was it pretty bad? People that didn't have no work in Spokane?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Desperate. They couldn't get a job, they couldn't get a dollar, they couldn't get credit. There was no relief, no unemployment, there was nothing. They were just on their own.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It didn't look like the right kind of life to me. I didn't figure on being in that bracket. I figured if I was going to be there, I'd have a job and I'd have some security. But, there's always this, there's always sickness, businesses that you might be in going broke and all those things. It didn't look good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do when you were working out?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I was involved with a plow company. I had a good job.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They'd get in a bunch of carload of machinery, farm equipment, and then it was our job to set it up and paint it and display it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Somebody from Palouse would send in a big order for repair parts and it was our job to go and dig them out of the bins and get them ready to ship.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Then the Oliver Plow Company decided that they would move to Portland and they wanted me to go with them. They wanted me to go with them. I wanted a good job down there. A little better than what I had.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But I thought I had seen enough. I went back to the woods.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I thought I had seen enough. I went back to the woods. I went back to the woods. I don't know who was right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lot of those people had come out of the country.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Most of them.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I think those kids that were raised in the city as competing with the kids that came in from the country, the country kids would forge ahead of them as a rule. They caught on quicker and adapted themselves more to new conditions faster than the kid that had been raised on the pavement.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe it's sort of like people coming from the east like the west so much because they don't take it for granted so much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what I mean?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If people come from the country, maybe they could see the city.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Maybe they could see what was going on a little more clearly. Yeah, there's that. But it was a matter of...

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, the country kid had always seen a revolving condition and the city kid had seen routine day after day the same. And with the country kid it never was two days alike. It was always changing. I think that's why they adapted faster. But I don't know if that's right. Well, from the point of view of the city person, the country life would look like it never changed probably the same day. That's right. It did. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you think it really did change.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It had a lot of change in it, living in the country. Oh, I think so. Just the seasonal change was enough.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Because you did something different in every season that came up in front of you.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And then... Oh, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you walk through those seasons with me and what it was like when you were a kid on the farm?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, I don't quite get you. Well, I mean, what was it...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're saying the seasonal work was a little different depending on the season.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I'm just wondering what it was like depending on... Well, what I meant by that, it was invariably changing. You put in the crop. If you watched it grow, you took care of the crop up to finishing point. And then you went on to something else. And then you harvested it. And then in the fall, you put in and started next year's crop. And during winter, I took care of the stock and got next winter's wood and maybe repaired or built new buildings. Oh, it was always just a continual round of pleasure. No money in it. Hard work. Plenty to eat.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: All we needed to wear didn't take much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You put the crop in in the fall?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It changed from day to day. It was just a constant change in a way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the seasons run the same way they do now or was it different?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Oh no, they can't change too much. You get light winters, cold winters, hot summers, dry summers, wet summers. Then, now, it's always, always, everlastingly, it's a little bit different.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I've heard a lot of people say here in the last month, we never had a spring like this.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They can't remember back to 1965, it was identical. I was 15th of June before I got ground dry enough to go out there and get more than two or three days at a time in the field.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But they're all different and they're all the same. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The winter came about the same time, more or less in the early days.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, fall might be stormy one year and clear the next, but winter was right behind it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: The seasons were there, they might be a little different each year, but not too much. It just seems like the weather is unpredictable here to me. Seems like you never know what's going to happen. Well, it's unpredictable every place except in the places where it's so dry they can't ever get any water and the wind blows continuously. Well, I don't know, I don't think it's any more unpredictable here than it is anyplace else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you just about never have crop failure here, right?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Never have had, that's something we haven't had. We've had years when the wheat froze and knocked our bushels down to, from 40 bushels or better, maybe to 15, shriveled feed stuff. But that's only happened once or twice in a lifetime. There have been years when we couldn't harvest all of our wheat because the rains had come and be so continual that we couldn't get into the field and the grain sprout standing up.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But that's only happened once or twice in a lifetime.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: We've had years where you cut your hay in the most beautiful weather and next thing you know the rain, rain, rain, you know, and you couldn't put your hay up. That's just rare occurrence.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But you always had a pretty fair production.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Not here. There's places where my wise folks settled in Dakota and one year gals took their crop and next year grasshoppers took their crop and the next year they were clear out here in Seattle. They didn't have any crop.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: That's happened to more areas than one, Kansas and all those areas had that. Montana's had it. We've came close but we've never had it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: 1926, I was working for Luke Egley and getting dollar a day. I was driving eight horses on the following day. I was going to be a little bit of a risk.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Every day and every day the wind blew and it was cold and there was a rain a few minutes and maybe there was snow a few minutes. And you'd put on all the clothes you could get but you'd stand up on that disk or plow and you'd just freeze. And when that season was over, I told Louie that I am never going to drive horses again for anybody else to put in a season of work. And I didn't either. I drove them for myself when the conditions weren't any better but it always seemed to me that whenever those conditions were bad, I never farmed over a couple hundred acres at any time in my life. I'd keep it rotated so if it was too darn bad, I didn't have to stay out there and take it. I could pull out and go do something else until the weather got better and then go back out and do it. On the other hand, there have been seasons when I was crowded with my work and I'd have to stay right out there and take it but it didn't seem bad to take it for myself as it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did most of the neighbors get by as far as making a living when there wasn't much money to be had? Did most of them work for Potlatch, most of your neighbors?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Oh, they worked someplace. Yeah, they did but they kept their cows, they kept their chickens, fogs, they got their own wood, they pumped their own water and a dollar's worth of kerosene and run them all winter.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They learned to be pretty well self -sustaining.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: These little farmers around this area used to go out in Walla Walla and Genesee and Colfax and harvest and make a hundred or two or three. They'd come back and with this little garden and milk cow and things they had, they had money enough to get by, they had all they needed.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: If a man had said that a family could live over the winter on $300 now, you'd ask him if he's alright today. You'd think he was crazy but it wasn't any problem.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most guys work out like you did seasonally?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: You worked out. It depends on the volume of their business and how they were set up. Anybody that was on less than 160 acres, pretty apt to. A few days at any rate.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But if a man could figure out some way to work for somebody or something to get enough to pay his taxes and kids school clothes and a few things, well he was alright.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Maybe he cut wood in the winter time. Maybe he could walk three or four miles and make ties or saw logs.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There was always some way. They got by beautifully. But you know they had imagination. This wouldn't work. They wouldn't quite make it. They could always invent something that would. They could.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There were potlice there within ten miles of town or even a little farther. Some members of the family worked in there as fathers or one of the boys or somebody. Women hardly ever, never you might say. They took care of the place. They milked. They fed the chickens and pigs.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There was always some place. A man making enough. He never was, they never were hungry. Never. Even during a depression.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: These farmers had enough to eat. It might not have been a whole variety they would have liked but they had enough. They were full. They weren't hungry.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Eggs, cream tacks, maybe two or three or five or six hogs. God it meant something. Now it's just gas money.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: When eggs and butter fat were eleven cents a dozen and a pound, we didn't have all we wanted but we evidently had all we needed because we were still here. That's a cinch.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: If a man had five or six darn good cows, don't matter whether they were eleven cents or thirty -five, we used to get in good times but it was survival. A hundred and fifty hens, same way.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: If a man had timber, he could take care of all those, the cattle and the garden and all those things. Maybe during a day he could go out and cut half a cord of wood. He could get three dollars for it after a while. Somebody would come along and buy it. I mean he'd get a dollar and a half for the half cord or three dollars for the whole cord. But somebody would come along and take it. They had an idea. They didn't have to have that dollar today. It was good anytime. When they could get it, if it was a year in the future, it was still good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From the guy that sold the wood too. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Even if he came from Colfax?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Even if he didn't know him by name?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He could buy wood or just about anything.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Barter, at times, was more important, relevant to a man's living than actual cash.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: That would be pretty nice if he could cut, I'd say ten cords of wood. And then I had a pretty nice heifer to the herd. Maybe she wouldn't go into production right away, but she was growing and she was going to produce after a while. He had a future there anyway.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I traded wood for just about everything.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Just about everything that a man has on his place at some time or other. Plows, discs, drills, hails, horses, cows, pigs, chickens, wheat.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They didn't have money and I didn't either. But we need these things. They were through with them. They had a new one or something. Maybe they bought it for $2 .50 and bring it up here and sell it to me for $12 .50. Fine and dandy. That was better than me giving $25 for it. New.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: So it was a fair deal. And I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mostly the trading was with people from Colfax and the polluter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could it be likely anybody?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm just wondering where most of the trading was done. Because it seemed like the people on the river there would have wood and the people down around Colfax would want to get it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They did. You know, these roads used to be lined like they are with cars. These roads used to be lined with teams and horses hauling wood.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They were just lined with it. About this time of year when the crop was in and before haying, they'd haul a few loads of wood. They'd come up today and back tomorrow.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And they had cash or they had something to trade or if they didn't have, they were acquainted as you and I are.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And they could trust each other and they did.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And it didn't matter what they brought up from down there, somebody could use it. And it didn't matter what they took back from up here, somebody could use it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Around potlatch there in 1930 to 1939, half the population cut wood, hauled it into potlatch. Got three dollars a cord and took it out in grocery store trading with the stores. Either Nonaway or Potlatch or Princeton or Harvard.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Three dollars a cord and they couldn't, one out of twenty of them, get three dollars in money. It was non -existent.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Oh, well, I don't know. It's been a good life. I wouldn't change it. I'd go right back and do it the same way I did.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It's pretty lucky.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It hasn't been easy but there's, I wasn't alone in that condition. There was hundreds just like me. But we were never hungry, we were never cold. And the damn settlement, we were unhappy.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: We might be worried but not for too long, I could figure out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that was the general rule?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Most people who were in the country were pretty satisfied with the way they were living.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I think it applied to all of us, yeah. Generally, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think Potlatch being there, the lumber company, made a real big difference to the lives of the small farmers in the countryside?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, they'd give them a market, they'd give them a closer town, they'd give them a railroad up the valley, they'd give them a job if they needed it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yes, it made a, it did a lot of good. Tremendous.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Lloyd Moles and I were working together one night and he said, you know, there isn't hardly anybody within 20 miles of here that hasn't pulled themselves out of some kind of a tight with a job with Potlatch. And I think he was, I think he was right.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But it gave, it brought a lot of advantage to the country, it was a lot of, uh, it was pretty nice to drive from the farm two or three miles to one of these little towns. It was quite a job to take something from way up on the head of the Palouse River, clear the Palouse to sell it, or go down there and get your groceries. And then back. Two days. They didn't go very often. They didn't go every day. By darn sight. Once a month.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, that railroad raised a standard of living and it benefited all through the years. Until it got roads and cars. And it, the whole picture changed there, it would go into a different cycle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the wages that Potlatch paid for the work done? Were they considered good by the people?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, there's only one wage, why, yeah, it's good.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: You could get a dollar a day working on a farm or you could get three dollars working up at Potlatch, but, uh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had a boss at Potlatch.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: You get your dollar a day and your room and board and laundry.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And if you were single, you'd have been making just as much or more with a dollar a day than if you went to Potlatch and got three dollars and paid board. That was a young guy. A married man that drove or rode a saddle horse ten miles to work in there.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He didn't have to pay any board to, uh, accepting that he was earning the groceries for the family.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There was a,

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: well I don't know, I don't think I considered it that way then, but I considered it kind of interesting now.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I looked back on it and changed the picture.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I worked with a fellow over there at Potlatch one winter when I had to go three miles to work. He went, uh, eight, three, twelve. He went twelve miles, night and morning. Went home to Carolee's team in the old four or five thousand. Went to bed and came right back the next morning. McLean, he lived way up on Deep Creek.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He didn't seem to be out of reason then, but you think back on it, my God, that man put up with something. Take him two hours to go, two hours to come, and then work eight hours, twelve hours. Take him three hours to eat breakfast and supper and do his few chores. He put in quite a day.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I wonder how many do that now.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He drove an open hack. He didn't have a cover over it or a heater in it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: So it must have been a lot of days that he was wet and cold and miserable when he got to work and the same way when he got home again.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And he wasn't the only one that done it. There was nothing.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But, yep, they made it and are pretty darn well fixed over there and well respected today. Either they or their children.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And I wonder if their grandkids would do it. I doubt it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that most of your neighbors where you grew up, those families, stayed for many years while you were growing up? Was there much turnover in a country when you were a kid?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Not a great deal, no. There'd be new ones come in. But they stayed. They didn't go again. Kids that I started school with the first day that I went to that particular school were right there when we finished it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But after they finished school then there weren't jobs around suitable for all of them. For one reason or another. Most of them left the area and went out. But there was a few of them that stayed there and made tremendous successes of themselves. There not only were financial successes but all other successes that go with it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I can name about a dozen of them to the country that died with half a million dollars.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: That's not much money in New York but that's a pretty darn stable piece of change out here in this country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about these people that would be moving in, the newcomers? Did they have to prove themselves? Were they accepted very easily?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They were accepted right now. I think they had to go ahead and prove themselves after that.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: When a family moved into the country, no, they were just as good as anybody. They accepted right off the bat.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And then they go ahead and prove what they could do. Most of them did pretty darn good.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: After those fellows got old enough to retire, then there was a change in the population because they left. They went to surrounding towns and retired. And turned the place over to some younger member of the family or sold it outright.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There'd be a time then when the population had changed. But that was just kind of a run of the mill condition.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you about the milk route that you had, the business that you had in Potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that during the Depression you did that?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, I bought it in the fall of 1928, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you buy?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, I bought the business and the livestock and the equipment that was on the place. Well, household goods.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I gave them about $3 ,000 and they just moved it off. That was my wife's folks. The furniture and everything was left there.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: By the fall of 1929, we had it just about half paid for. And then this Depression came on. They laid the mill crew off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They laid what off?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: The mill crew. That was my customers. I'll be eternally damned. It took us the next 20 years to make what we had made in that one year and gain.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: The mill crew didn't have any money. Some of them could cut wood. Some of them would come out and help me with my haying. And a few of them worked. The railroad crew, the office crew, the supervisory. I had enough of them to keep me solvent. And then I had customers in there that couldn't make a dime. They couldn't get a job at anything. And I just kept right on delivering their milk. If they owed me a lot, I might as well do that as take 11 cents for it. So I did it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: By the time we got to 1935, I sold the business to my brother. In fact, I just practically gave it to him. And I took my cattle and horses and machinery and moved up on that place at Harvard.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And people would pay me two or three dollars or a dollar or two or five dollars or two. And until I, after it all wound up, I lost 20 dollars on one man. And one man only out of all the people I dealt with in Potlice beat me out of 20 dollars. And he did it to everybody. He just moved out of town. One night.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But by some huck or quick, the rest of them squared every dime of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't imagine you kept reminding them either.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They knew it themselves that they owed you the money. I never done any of them for a dollar or a dime. Never.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They'd cut wood. Say, Glenn, I got a cord of wood. And I was finding Dandy and Lige Brown maybe or somebody else with Lige Brown from Thornton out there between Spokane and Colfax. He'd be up in a few days. And there'd be two or three of them have a cord of wood if he'd load them up, take them to Thornton and sell it. He'd be back in a few days. He either had nine dollars or maybe a horse. Didn't matter.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Truckload of harness.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Anything. An old sow.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Anything. It didn't matter. He could bring up anything he wanted to. Somebody'd want it. Mother -in -law. Mother -in -law. I was selling about 125 quarts of milk and getting 13 and a half cents a quart. I think I bought 14 or 15 cows from them and had one or two that I took with me. Three horses and farmed machine me enough to farm that place.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And I had another place across the river with just about the same kind of an outfit on it, but I had an auction sale and sold it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And put the money of my outfit into the dairy outfit. I borrowed all my insurance, borrowed a little more on my outfit. But anyway, I had enough to get a hold of it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And then start digging. Two people never worked any harder than we two did there for those five or six years we were there. I'd take care of the milk and deliver the milk. I can just take care of the cooking and wash the bottles, wash the buckets.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It was a tremendous job, but we struggled through. We never had any help from anybody.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: All the rest of the help around that area were on WPA or anything we could get.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: We just struggled through it. If we didn't have it, we'd get along without it until we had it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: We'd have to make a few dollars, you know. And about the time we got $100 or $200 a head, we needed hay, we needed bottles, we needed bottle caps.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: We needed solvents to clean the bottles and dishes with. So we'd get all these things we had to have and by the time we got them all picked up, we were $100 short. So $100 out was the charge. We'd start in and dig again until we got that paid up and then we'd get another $100 or $200 and think, oh boy, we're just really saving long. And bottom would drop out of everything. We needed everything again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it you told me about the team when you were in Potlatchy that team would sort of take care of itself? Drive themselves?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, I'd drive up in front of the house and a little girl running jumped on the sled and the team heard that rattle. They took off at a dead run, run down half a block and stopped like that. The next customer.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And I'd take probably a dozen quarts of milk and go around the block this way or through the block and that team would go around and come down here to the next stop and stop. And they did it all over town. They knew it. They knew it as well as I did. And old Jess, I'd been gone from that milk rut about four years. And I was driving her through town on the highway and had come to Les Flasher's house and that old mare pulled in and stopped four years after she'd come off that milk rut.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: You know that team would be going around the block one way and I'd be going the other way and people would stop them and hold them until I come back and found them. People that didn't know, you know, what was going on. Strangers.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: No, I don't know just what extent a horse could think and figure but a whole lot more than they ever kept credit for. The funny part was when they'd move from stop to stop, they'd move in a good brisk trot. They wouldn't walk. They'd just say right along. And when they got to the right place, they'd just freeze and stop.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I had eight head on a plow up there at Harvard and a neighbor of the Webster boys came over there and they crawled up on the plow and walked along. I just hitched the lines around a plow lever and rolled a cigarette. And they said, you've got that team pretty well trained to do that. Yeah, I thought they were. I never touched those lines until I come back to the same place. They made that complete four corners on that field and never missed an inch. And I never touched them.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They'd been doing it month in and month out, year in and year out.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Just as natural as someone's breathing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You must have seen pretty much how people were doing in Potlatch during the Depression when you were on that route. I mean going house to house and delivering. At least you must have gotten some idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it really tough for a lot of the people there in that town? Or did they?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, it really was. They were struggling just as hard as I was or harder. They were going without. Although they finally got this Public Works Progress project started and that helped. That was groceries. I think they were, 90 % of them were way behind on the rent.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They didn't dress too fancy.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They done the best they could and I admire them yet. I've got friends over there yet. If anybody says anything about Glenn Gilder that isn't just right up to snuff, they'll still fight for me.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And I've got others over there that would swear I've never done a decent thing in my life. I expect most everybody has.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's hard for me to believe you have any enemies over there, Glenn.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I don't think I have. That's something I've never had a whole lot of. And I've had a couple of three that were pretty bitter towards me. Until they got things figured out. It might take them 10 years but they figured out and they're pretty good friends. Now if they're still alive they're pretty good friends.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I never bothered anybody. I never, I always sooner felt mad I walked away from a man. I wasn't afraid of him. I never been afraid of anything in my life. But I always figured out it was wrong to write back up to a point. I never had to a great deal. But if anybody, if I figured if anybody was trying to really slip something over on me, I wouldn't hesitate to tell him what I thought about it. It generally didn't cause too much hard feelings.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It generally knew in their own mind that I was right or I wouldn't say anything. But I made mistakes and I made mistakes in the same trend. I misinterpret somebody's actions or attitudes or words.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It generally figured them out. It generally boiled down. Great job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Reminds me of the guy George was talking about when we were up talking with him. That guy that he said he knew he couldn't trust at all. He stole from him and you said you just knew that guy well enough to watch him. You watched him real close.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He remembered.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Oh they were rare.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's good. I would think in a community where people have been there and know each other real well, you couldn't have that kind of thing very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A guy who would be like that, how could he live there?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Because everybody would know what he was. That's right.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: You do something wrong and the punishment was right in your own mind. It wasn't in what the other people thought of it. It was in your own mind because you knew what they thought.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: So that kept people, all of us, pretty straight. We just tried to do right. But we didn't.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: We didn't have this condition that we've got now. There's so many people around and so few knowing about all of them that they don't seem to give a tinker's damn. But then we were close together. We knew everybody intimately for miles around.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Our old horses would be driving down the road and our old horses met another team. They'd pull out and when they got right beside them they'd stop and talk. It didn't matter whether they knew him or not or whether the man who was driving them knew him or not. That's what they'd do. Maybe they'd talk for two minutes or maybe two hours. But they did. And that's why that whole country, and all these countries around here, were acquainted from end to end. Meet in the stores, meet in the churches, meet in the school programs, meet on the roads.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now that there sounds like a real difference between the country and the city.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Spokane, who would you know? You probably knew the guys you were working with and who else?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: That's all. That's all. Or maybe the people he loved to it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I think I look back on it, I perhaps was more or less lonesome in town. How it could be I don't know because I could go out here and stay in a logging camp or an old mine shack for two weeks and never see a soul on earth. And I never got lonesome.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Why would I get lonesome with a thousand people around me?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Hi! Hi! It didn't sound like it was on tape.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do for entertainment in Spokane then? Anyway, what was there to do?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Go out in the country rabbit hunting. That was one thing. You can do that in the country. Go to a dance if that was what we wanted to do that night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a movie?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Go to a show. Go to the fights. I like to go down to the Golden Gloves Club and watch those fights. Oh no, there was entertainment.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But $22 or $3 a week and you pay your board and all this entertainment came out of your pocket and you just didn't have it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: So we did enjoy ourselves I'm pretty sure.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: When you wanted a new shirt you saved up to get it and it would probably take you two weeks to save that much.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: No,

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I liked it out in the country because you didn't need a new shirt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you do at work?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Usual. Turn that thing off. You've got an extra man around there that he didn't like and he's trying to get this extra man canned. And as a result of it they kind of got into a little bit of a strike and I was on a day crew and my crew was on a day crew. Winmire was on the graveyard and Honeycamp was on the afternoon. And this Winmire kept agitating things until he got the crew all together and they was to go up and talk to the chief engineer and get this problem solved. And as long as Winmire had instigated the whole thing I thought he was responsible to go and do as everybody else. I made a circle around my pliers and down in the pump room and seen engines light up into the big engine room and over at the stairway that went up the turbine room and there was Winmire alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, what's the score Harry? That you're not up there? I said, the other boys all going home?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: No, they're up there in the turbine room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, how come you're not up there?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, Jim think they needed me. I popped him right between the horns and he backed off and I went up and gave him another. The engineer was standing right behind me and I thought, sure I'm canned but I don't give a damn if that son of a bitch is going to instigate this strike and let the other guys take the blame for it. It ain't right and I popped him two or three times and he took off and he ran up the goddamn stairs and he went clear into the rest of the boys.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well that was my long change coming up so I, nothing said. At three o 'clock I went home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The next day I came back up to the grocery store for some groceries and the chief engineer walked in and said, Gilder, did you hit Winmire?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I said, you're goddamn right.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: What?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well I said, you know why, I'm not going to tell you. I said, yes, that's all they told him. He said, Gilder, I want you to come down tomorrow morning on Winmire shift and take your job. It's the first time that anybody ever got a promotion in the fight. I was fighting.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But it just peed me off so bad that he just had stirred this whole damn thing up and then didn't have guts enough to follow through and go through with him and go up and talk to the boys. I didn't care whether they canned me or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gave you his job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it he stirred up? I mean what was the trouble over?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: He wanted a certain man canned.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: God, the man that he wanted canned was the best man to stew with him.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But my crew went along with them. We talked it over before and we said go up and see that both sides of it are aired up there, not just one side.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had a rule that you'd be canned if you started a fight?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They kept it. They enforced it. I think to this day I'm the only man that ever got promoted out of a fight in the mail fight.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I didn't care whether they canned me or not. I knew I'd live. I hadn't been married six months but I was still concerned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your job? What was the job?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I was faring at the time but when I came back I came back onto the water tender. I've got quite a raise in pay. Windmire went down the road and I got a promotion.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But when he jumped me there and he spoke so sharp I thought, oh boy, here it comes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was about the same time you were talking to the doctor when you were working for the mill then?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I was talking to the doctor quite a bit before that, probably a year.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I worked down there off and on. I worked down there for five years I think. Off and on. I stayed steady after I got in the boiler room. I stayed there. I was after that engineer's qualifications and I got it. But I never used the third pot lights. I quit. I went and bought the dairy and then I never used it until after I'd got rid of the dairy.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I moved up to Harvard I guess and then I run the engines over at Karskalins and different places, boons.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: And then about the time I got a good job I was there at Karskalins. Electric planet. There wasn't any more steam. Engineers needed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that logging of a sawmill at Karskalins?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, running a sawmill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see that big Corliss engine in pot lights there?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: It's in the boiler room in the power plant. I've heard of it. That old flywheel is just about 20 feet from top to bottom. When the battle goes on.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I've started and stopped that thing I expect a hundred times but I never did blow the whistle. I never got the blow whistle caught.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: That was strictly the engineer's job to blow that whistle and it was strictly the engineer's job to start this Corliss.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There were three engineers on there on a three shift and I had known them all my life. They said I want to start it this morning and they said go ahead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it take much to start it up?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Just a little routine. You had to open the bleeder valves and see if all the lubricators were open and open the throttle. Close the bleeder valves and let her gradually work up to speed and stand there the rest of the day and watch it. Nothing to it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But they were particular. I mean I had to know his business so they wouldn't let him run them on them.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: They didn't dare. State law is pretty strict on it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Should be strict on so many diesel runs too but they're not too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see any injuries on a plant while you were working for Potlatch? Did you see men get hurt?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: No. The fact is all the time I work for them across gallons either. I'm the only one that ever got hurt in there. I was cleaning off sediment and dust that forms over the top of the boilers and on the decks and all around. We take the steam hose and get up there and blow that all off. You know, clean it all off. And I dropped the steam hose and it just went to whipping like that and it hit me on the feet here and it just cracked it and burned me to it. I finally got around and got a hold of it and got a stop and told Vern, I said, Vern, I got a pretty bad burn and I think I better go to the doctor. He took about a half a look and he said, gee, gods, I think you had. Oh, they rushed me in. It was just a little, from about there up to here, kind of streaked and striped on both legs. I never laid off or lost any time. I just go in and get a dress and go back to work. Hurt like a devil for a day or two. I never seen any of the men get hurt in the mill, although there were some that got hurt there during that time. And up there at Moscow, I got this hand caught in the chain. And down there at Rousies, I got it caught in the car door and lost a finger. I was a little scared, but...

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Unavoidable as it could be, but... Well, I guess the only way to avoid it is just not been there.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I was doing my job both times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There were some pretty serious accidents there.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, one of the men killed, Larry Potter, oiling out in the mill, reached out over the belt off this Corliss engine to the drive shaft up in the mill. And something caught in his clothes and drug him in there and he went around that, between the belt and that wheel. And hell, it was 250 pounds of pressure to the inch on that belt. I imagine it just flattened him out. I didn't see it.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: There was a fellow fell into the slicer saws over there and the slicer saws were 16 inches apart and they're about that high. He was laying cross -rays of that damn thing. How he ever got out of the lab, I don't know. He cut off an arm and part of a foot.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Somebody seemed to stop the chain or he would have just been cut up in little pieces.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Well, there's been bad accidents around the mill. It used to be considered real high -rated hazardous.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: But, anymore, they've got the money and they've got the laws and they've got the safety inspection that isn't enforced too much, but no little. And modern machinery that keeps people out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, hey Glenn, what did you say that Frank's father used to cut his little toe off?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I could use a pocket knife. I might have used a straight edge razor, but I doubt it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he use for? He used alcohol or what did he use to cauterize?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: I think Frank said he used carbolic acid. Put a little of it in the water and put his foot in the pan of water a little while and got up and walked off. So nothing would happen.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Here's some coffee. Dinner's almost ready.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I see we got a little sugar for Glenn.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Oh, okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you use milk?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Yep, you have it handy. Can of milk. I think we're out of milk. Well, it is fine this way. Okay. Just forget me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Forget you, huh?

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: This is the way where I get it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That corn must have been bothering him pretty bad.

speaker: Glen Gilder
words: Oh, the kind of things can hurt. There I have it.
